item.title: Deep Dive Into Domains Of Dread With Ravenloft: The Horrors Within
item.content:encoded:
Ravenloft: The Horrors Within is releasing in just a few weeks. With it, Dungeons & Dragons is diving once again into the world of TTRPG horror, giving DMs information about the varying Domains of Dread, stat blocks for the powerful Darklords, and character options designed to inform story and roleplay, as well as mechanical benefit.

Ahead of its release, I got invited to hear from Greg Bilsland, Executive Producer of Dungeons & Dragons, Wes Schneider, Game Design Lead for Ravenloft: The Horrors Within, Makenzie De Armas, Game Designer for Ravenloft: The Horrors Within, with a particular focus on player options, and AJ Hanneld, Senior Creative Lead of Dungeons & Dragons.

As always, I like to start with the pure numbers of what Dungeons & Dragons will be including in their new book. Across 288 pages of content for Players and DMs alike, the players.

For the DM, you'll have

  • Content highlighting the Domains of Dread
  • Stat Blocks for creatures, including 17 Dark Lords and 51 other horrors or potential allies
  • 17 Adventures
  • 8 Bastion Facilities
  • 46 Maps
  • Tools to create your own Domains of Dread

For players, you'll be able to craft spooky characters with

  • 7 Subclasses, including the Reanimator Artificer and the Hollow Warden Ranger
  • 4 Species, including the Dhampir, Reborn, and Lupine
  • 11 feats in total, including 2 Origin Feats and 9 Dark Gifts that add mechanical benefit, while also including serious drawbacks…
  • 4 Backgrounds, including the Haunted, Investigator, Wanderer, and Spirit Medium

How Is The Horrors Within Different From Van Richten's?

Early in our discussion, as if to pre-empt a member of the press asking, the comparison was quickly drawn between what would be featured in this book and how it would be different from 2021's Van Richten's Guide To Ravenloft. In Schneider's explanation, he explained that "The lens for Ravenloft: The Horrors Within is different. Van Richten presented the broader Domains of Dread, outside of Barovia in Curse of Strahd; here was the opportunity to look at more 'What's the foundation? What are the horror baselines?"

"This isn't just a setting book, this is about what fear, obsession,a nd grief can do to players, monsters, and dark lords" - Schneider

Listening to the community feedback from the last delving into horror, the team came away with the idea that players had enough tools that let them know about the physical places that were the Domains of Dread, but they wanted to do more to better equip DMs with the tools to better understand horror and to equip them with tools to torture their players, create meaningful adventures.

The best example they could give for empowering DMs was to give the Darklords their own proper statblocks. While they're a terrifying fight, it does put these Darklords in a mechanically reachable place.

Ravenloft: The Horrors Within will include Cthulhu as a cosmic creature that the party can encounter or even face in the expanded look at Cosmic/Eldritch horror. Cthulhu, as you might expect, is the dark lord of the Domain of Dread, Innsmouth. While there was a joke thrown out about whether this was "just a city from Massachusetts moved into a Domain of Dread," Schneider did say this was its own D&D spin on the famous setting, making sure, just like the other planes, it would be infused with magic.

Ravenloft: The Horrors Within Player Choices Inform Stories

De Armas took over to highlight some of the approaches that were taken to creating horror-themed player options that didn't just provide mechanical benefits, but also helped to inform and shape the character's development.

I got told to make it as gross and disgusting as possible, then turned it in and was told to dial it back down" - De Armas

Speaking to two of the new Sub-Classes, more was revealed about the Reanimator Artificer and the Hollow Warden Ranger. For the Reanimator, they wanted to keep the crafting/builder archetype but make it 'fleshier and more grotesque' than previously released subclasses. The Reanimator will have an undead companion that can die and be revived. It was also teased that as the Reanimator levels up, they'll have the option to add modifications to alter them mechanically and make them even more unsettling to describe.

For players who use D&D Beyond, it was also explained that if you purchase this book digitally, but you didn't purchase Eberron: Forge of the Artificer, you'll get access to the Artificer class with your purchase of Ravenloft.

The Hollow Warden Ranger is the Dungeons & Dragons approach to a Folk Horror ranger. These rangers will draw their power from eldritch beings and cryptids. Direct inspiration was taken from myths out of Appalachia.

One of the new Dark Gifts gives your character a slightly fish-like look, complete with gills to tie into an Innsmouth-style story. While there might be a benefit to your ability to hold your breath underwater, your new look could make charismatic situations just that harder to deal with. These Gifts are meant to function as "horror story prompts that springboard you into making horror decisions."

Crafting The Visual Design Of Ravenloft: The Horrors Within

Throughout the presentation, as we got to see artwork of Darklords, new Domains of Dread, and representations of the different Dark Gifts, Hanneld was able to walk us through how the design and art teams built the rules and language of the recurring themes and colors present throughout the book.

There was a distinct desire to move away from the Black/White/Brown of traditional horror. Inspiration was taken from more contemporary horror films from makers like A24 and Blumhouse, like I Saw The TV Show and Neon Demon. Each Domain of Dread was designed with two palettes of colors in mind; a Domain like Barovia will show off purples and pinks, while Innsmouth art will have teal and green.

Hanneld worked closely with artists and made sure that the color 'language,' as well as a request for 'weird' angles, were implemented to make sure all of the art would be consistent and cohesive throughout the book. It was highlighted that for Ravenloft: The Horrors Within, they've increased what they're paying artists, and they've immediately seen that they're getting even better artwork from it.

Ravenloft Offerings Beyond The Book

In addition to Ravenloft: The Horrors Within, there are additional products being released to work with your horror sessions, as well as those more tangentially linked to the release.

For the DM who wants to add more physical props to the game, a Tarokka deck will be getting released, featuring 56 cards of artwork from 15 different artists. The deck comes with rules for how to implement the deck into your game as a way to roleplay. It also includes information on how to stack the deck so that it can be something to narratively inform your play.

In addition to being released physically, the Tarokka deck will be added digitally as a D&D Beyond Drop. Subscribers will get access to this in late June, but it will also be digitally purchasable separately.

A DM Screen is being released for Ravenloft. The artwork on the back features four different vignettes from different Domains of Dread, again conforming to the color language of the group of products. On the inside, you'll find your standard information, in the new 'for laptop' format, with conditions, actions, skills, etc. This DM screen also has a table with horror encounters that can be worked into play, which can be picked, rolled for, or even drawn from the Tarokka deck.

Additional products are also in development for Ravenloft: The Horrors Within outside Wizards of the Coast, with products releasing froVanm partners like Ravensburger, Displate, Wizkids, Sirius, and Hot Topic.

We were shown product photos for Sirius dice for Strahd, Darklords, and the new Warlock and Ranger. New WizKids miniatures to show off a variety of creatures that will be featured in the book, a Ravenloft edition of D&D: Horrified, and T-shirts and Sweaters featuring Strahd and Cthulhu.

Ravenloft: The Horrors Within is releasing on June 16th, with those subscribing to the Master Tier of D&D Beyond able to access it today, and those at the Hero Tier able to access it on June 9th.

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/news/deep-dive-into-domains-of-dread-with-ravenloft-horrors-within

item.title: You Can Now Listen To Animal Crossing Background Music In Your Browser, While You Work, Hour-By-Hour, All Day Long
item.content:encoded:
I’ve had the Nintendo Music app downloaded for a while now, but I’ve barely used it. There are just very few scenarios in my day-to-day life where the music I want to listen to via my portable telephone is, like, Mario sounds. But just last night, Nintendo finally, finally, added a Nintendo Music web browser player feature, and you know what that means!

That’s right: 24/7 Animal Crossing hourly music on my desktop.

I listen to stuff with lyrics in the car or while I’m doing chores, but at my while computer working, I need instrumental stuff to be able to focus, and Animal Crossing music is the perfect, relaxing, innocuous soundtrack for a day of writing. Nintendo music has a ton of Animal Crossing playlists available, with music from the original Animal Crossing, the 3DS’s New Leaf, and the Switch’s New Horizons, including three playlists for each game that include all the hourly music for every weather type: sunny, rainy, snowy. The “hourly” music is the background music that plays when you’re outdoors in each game. It changes on the hour, every hour, so you can just listen to this soothing background music on loop all day.

But the best part is that Nintendo added a big button a while back to each playlist that reads, “Play Hourly Tracks.” If you select this while on any of the Sunny Day, Rainy Day, or Snowy Day playlists, it will automatically play and loop the appropriate music for each hour of the day, changing it on the hour, every hour. I’ve been listening to the Sunny Day tracks for New Leaf since 8am today and am having an excellent time. I might switch to New Horizons toward the end of the day, just because there’s nothing quite like that moment when the 5pm music hits and you know it’s time to log off.

If you’re feeling a little nostalgic for Animal Crossing but don’t have the time to break the game out and reintroduce yourself to all your villagers, I highly recommend digging into the web browser app today instead and enjoying the upbeat, nicely repetitive sounds of the hourly themes. Now if Nintendo would just put Ring Fit Adventure music on here for workouts, my entire day would be solved.



https://kotaku.com/animal-crossing-music-nintendo-web-browser-app-2000701527

item.title: LoL Patch 26.12 Will Bring Huge Changes Ahead of MSI 2026
item.content:encoded:
Riot Games has released a preview for the next League of Legends update, Patch 26.12, which will be the last patch before the Mid-Season Invitational (MSI).

League of Legends MSI 2026 will be then be played on Patch 26.13, and it seems Riot is making significant changes before then with the large international esports tournament in mind.

The preview reveals that at least 15 champions will receive buffs or nerfs in the next patch, though these are subject to change as Riot is still collecting data from 26.11.

And there is also set to be an update to Teleport, which could have huge repercussions for pro-level games.

All of this is coming to the game, plus a huge ARAM Mayhem update and the introduction of Ranked 5s as revealed by the latest LoL dev update.

Changes Coming Expected in LoL Patch 26.12

  • Buffs
    • Yuumi
    • Jax
    • Gwen
    • Aatrox
    • Hwei
    • Syndra
    • Tristana
    • Sylas
  • Nerfs
    • xin Zhao
    • Nocturne
    • Lee Sin
    • Orianna
    • Ryze
    • Cassiopeia
    • Varus
  • System Adjustments
    • Teleport

Phroxzon Explains 26.12 Changes

Lead gameplay designer Matt ‘Phroxzon’ Leung-Harrison has broken down the proposed changes for the next patch.

Phroxzon revealed that the publishers aren’t too happy with instances in pro play where Teleport is used just for its shield:

“While this is a cool usage of the spell, it’s a pretty unintuitive behaviour, and we want to skew its usage to be better to use for its cross-map and joining fights capabilities,” the designer said.

As a result, Riot is looking to decrease the duration of the shield while making the shield a little bigger in compensation.

Making the shield bigger does sound counterintuitive, but it will depend on the final numbers.

Additionally, he revealed that Yuumi is getting compensation buffs for the Moonstone nerfs, and Lee Sin is being nerfed slightly after the changes to his kit in 26.10.

However, the main focus for this patch appears to be shaking up the mid lane meta as Phroxzon explained:

“For average play, AP Xin Zhao has been tearing up the rift with a lot of his power budget in healing, especially in the early lane creating some pretty uninteractive lane states.”

“Otherwise, [expect] a few adjustments to the champions who have a bit of room in Solo Queue, like Hwei, Sylas, Syndra and nerfs to some of the top-end like Cassio (who really benefited from Deathfire Touch being added), Ryze and Ori who have been strong for pretty much the whole season.”

What do you make of the proposed changes? Let us know on the Insider Gaming Discord.

Meanwhile, Riot has also revealed when the new T1 Worlds 2025 winners’ skins and next Hall of Fame skins will release.

item.title: Marathon Devs Reveal ‘Sponsored Survival’, a PvP-Lite Mode
item.content:encoded:
Marathon’s Season 2 goes live today, and with it, players get a new PvP-lite-focused experimental mode, Sponsored Survival, with lower risk.

Players have been waiting for the new season for some time, especially since the developers announced that Marathon would receive two new experimental PvE modes in the new season. A new experimental mode is now already live and will remain accessible until June 9. Check out how it works below:

Marathon’s New Experimental Mode Starts as PvE, But Soon Lets Rooks In

Bungie has published a new blog post introducing players to the experimental Marathon game mode, Sponsored Survival. In this mode, players find themselves alone on the map, which is set in the new Night Marsh Zone. This allows them to explore and loot freely without the distraction of other players. However, it won’t be completely danger-free, as players will still encounter UESC, which can be quite challenging, along with new enemies to face later as well.

After a period of time, backfilled Rooks will enter the map. They can choose to team up with others or take on the map alone. The objective of this mode is to survive until the final exfiltration, with matches running on an 18-minute timer. Once the timer reaches zero, players must rush for the final exfil. However, things change at this point. During the exfil, players will have two options: they can either negotiate a temporary truce to exfiltrate together or turn their weapons on each other in the dead of night.

In other news, in a bid to save Destiny, fans have rallied behind a Destiny 3 petition, and it’s accrued more signatures than Marathon’s peak player count. Additionally, Marathon’s failure to deliver has had a massive impact on Sony and Bungie, with a $765 million impairment loss being recorded. What are your thoughts on Marathon’s Sponsored Survival experimental mode? Leave your thoughts down in the comments, and join the official Insider Gaming Discord server.

For more information from Insider Gaming, read about ARC Raiders coming to Nintendo but not Switch 2. Don’t forget to sign up for our weekly newsletter.

item.title: ARC Raiders Comes to Nintendo, But Not Switch 2
item.content:encoded:
ARC Raiders remains a sensational breakout extraction adventure title, even if sentiments have slipped a bit in the last couple of months. It maintains solid player retention and something of an aura, but some of Embark Studios’ latest changes have left players wanting something a little more meaningful.

On social media, images have cropped up showing how far some ARC Raiders players will go to drive the innovation of this game further, bringing their own meaningful changes into the picture. What follows is the tale of how one fan managed to get ARC Raiders working on a Nintendo 3DS XL.

ARC Raiders, But on Nintendo, But Retro

ARC Raiders is currently not available on Nintendo Switch 2, and there’s no indication that it ever will be. While Embark Studios is slowing down major updates and preparing for a Chinese version to hit the market, fans have taken it upon themselves to push the limits of the game as far as possible.

On Reddit, one user shared images of ARC Raiders playing on a 3DS XL console, and by all accounts, it works just fine.

But there’s a major twist, and that’s that the game isn’t running natively on the classic handheld device.

The user, YeezysKanye2020, was using an application called Moonlight to stream ARC Raiders from his desktop PC, powered by an RTX 5070 GPU, to the 3DS XL. Moonlight’s self-written description professes how the app can ‘allow you to play your PC games on almost any device,’ and that certainly rings true in this situation.

The unidentified user even managed to pair a Steam Controller to get away from the 3DS XL’s fiddly touch controls, just to make life easier. He stressed that ‘FPS isn’t actually the problem’, but the controls were the biggest blocker to extraction-based success on Nintendo’s 14-year-old handheld console.

Unfortunately, things turned a little awry (and I ceased advising to pursue replication) when YeezysKanye2020 admitted that a hacked 3DS XL is also needed to run ARC Raiders.

But still, the innovation of some gamers knows no bounds.

Do you wish ARC Raiders would come to Nintendo officially? Let us know on the Insider Gaming Discord server.

For more Insider Gaming coverage, check out the news that Fallout 76 current-gen versions are being tested

Senior Editor and Esports Lead

Grant has been gaming for 30+ years and in the industry for 10+. You'll probably find him playing a post-apocalyptic game or an extraction shooter somewhere.

More from Grant Taylor-Hill

item.title: LEGO Pokémon Expands With 12 Smart Play Sets, Featuring Pikachu, Charizard and More
item.content:encoded:
Snap.

LEGO has announced its long-expected line-up of Pokémon sets that take advantage of its new Smart Play technology, with a dozen designs featuring Mewtwo, Charizard, Pikachu and more.

All 12 sets are available to pre-order now ahead of their launch on August 1. Every set is Smart Play-enabled, though you'll need one of the two core 'All-in-One' sets with Smart Bricks included to access the technology yourself.

In general, the range is focused on buildable models of Pokémon species that can be used as toys themselves — rather than being intricate dioramas you place on a shelf, or anything scaled to Minifigure size. Interactivity will let you hear Pikachu burble with happiness as he eats a sandwich, or Charizard laugh as you tickle him — powered by the Smart Brick, which detects movement and color and emits light and sounds.

Prices range from $19.99 for a couple of the smaller sets, up to $120 for the 751-piece Charizard vs. Jolteon Ultimate Battle set, which features two Smart Bricks.

To celebrate the launch, LEGO stores in the U.S., U.K., Germany, France, Poland and Australia will become PokéStops locations within Pokémon Go this August, where creatures with unique LEGO backgrounds can be encountered. In-game stickers and avatar items can also be unlocked.

LEGO Pokémon Smart Play Sets

Spending more than $130 on the range will unlock the LEGO Pokémon Smart Play: Ditto as Squirtle: Movie Night set, while stocks last. Details of the full range lie below:

LEGO Pokémon SMART Play: All-in-One sets

LEGO Pokémon SMART Play: Training House with Pikachu (72164), All-in-one set

LEGO SMART Play™: Training House with Pikachu

  • Pieces: 400
  • Dimensions: The treehouse, training dummy and bush in this 400-piece building toy measures over 8 in. (20 cm) high, 11 in. (27 cm) wide and 4 in. (11 cm) deep
    • Includes 1 SMART Brick, 1 SMART Charger, 4 SMART Tags, 1 figure.
  • Price: USD 69.99 / EUR 69.99 / GBP 59.99

LEGO Pokémon SMART Play: Charizard vs. Jolteon Ultimate Battle (72167), All-in-one set

LEGO SMART Play™: Charizard vs. Jolteon Ultimate Battle

  • Pieces: 751
  • Dimensions: Charizard figure stands over 7 in. (18 cm) tall, while Jolteon stands over 5 in. (12 cm) tall
    • Includes 2 SMART Bricks, 4 Tags, 2 Figures & Charger
  • Price: USD 119.99 / EUR 119.99 / GBP 109.99

LEGO Pokémon SMART Play: Compatible Sets

LEGO Pokémon SMART Play: Berry Bash with Bulbasaur and Bidoof (72155)

LEGO SMART Play™: Berry Bash with Bulbasaur and Bidoof

  • Pieces: 240
  • Dimensions: Brick-built Bulbasaur figure measures over 2.5 in. (6 cm) high, 1.5 in. (4 cm) wide and 2.5 in. (7 cm) deep
    • Includes 2 SMART Tags and 2 Figures
  • Price: USD 19.99 / EUR 19.99 / GBP 17.99

LEGO Pokémon SMART Play: Trainer's Buggy Adventure with Squirtle (72156)

LEGO SMART Play™: Trainer's Buggy Adventure with Squirtle

  • Pieces: 320
  • Dimensions: Buggy measuring over 4.5 in. (12 cm) high, 7.5 in. (19 cm) long and 3.5 in. (9 cm) wide
    • Includes 2 SMART Tags and 1 Pokémon
  • Price: USD 29.99 / EUR 29.99 / GBP 24.99

LEGO Pokémon SMART Play: Charmander and Geodude's Cavern Clash (72157)

LEGO SMART Play™: Charmander and Geodude's Cavern Clash

  • Pieces: 198
  • Dimensions: Charmander in this 198-piece building toy measures over 3 in. (7 cm) high, 2.5 in. (6 cm) wide and 3 in. (7 cm) deep
    • Includes 2 SMART Tags and 2 Pokémon
  • Price: USD 19.99 / EUR 19.99 / GBP 17.99

LEGO Pokémon SMART Play: Sprigatito, Fuecoco and Quaxly Battle (72158)

LEGO SMART Play™: Sprigatito, Fuecoco and Quaxly Battle

  • Pieces: 313
  • Dimensions: Quaxly figure in this 313-piece building toy stands over 3.5 in. (8 cm) tall
    • Includes 3 SMART Tags and 3 Pokémon
  • Price: USD 34.99 / EUR 34.99 / GBP 29.99

LEGO Pokémon SMART Play: Jigglypuff Concert (72159)

LEGO SMART Play™: Jigglypuff Concert

  • Pieces: 88
  • Dimensions: Measures over 2 in. (5 cm) high, 2.5 in. (6 cm) wide and 5 in. (5 cm) deep
    • Includes 1 SMART Tag and 1 Pokémon
  • Price: USD 14.99 / EUR 14.99 / GBP 12.99

LEGO Pokémon SMART Play: Drone Search for Mythical Mew (72161)

LEGO SMART Play™: Drone Search for Mythical Mew

  • Pieces: 429
  • Dimensions: Measures over 4 in. (11 cm) high, 3.5 in. (9 cm) wide and 3 in. (8 cm) deep
    • Includes 3 SMART Tags and 1 Pokémon
  • Price: USD 49.99 / EUR 49.99 / GBP 44.99

LEGO Pokémon SMART Play: Eevee and Lapras's Treasure Hunt (72162)

LEGO SMART Play™: Eevee and Lapras's Treasure Hunt

  • Pieces: 623
  • Dimensions: Includes Lapras, measuring over 6 in. (16 cm) high, 6.5 in. (17 cm) wide and 5 in. (13 cm) deep
    • Includes 3 SMART Tags and 2 Pokémon
  • Price: USD 59.99 / EUR 59.99 / GBP 54.99

LEGO Pokémon SMART Play: Mewtwo's Lab Break (72163)

LEGO SMART Play™: Mewtwo's Lab Break

  • Pieces: 605
  • Dimensions: The Mewtwo figure in this 605-piece building toy stands over 7.5 in. (19 cm) tall
    • Includes 1 SMART Tag and 1 Pokémon
  • Price: USD 69.99 / EUR 69.99 / GBP 59.99

LEGO Pokémon SMART Play: Umbreon vs. Garchomp Championship Battle (72165)

LEGO SMART Play™: Umbreon vs. Garchomp Championship Battle

  • Pieces: 831
  • Dimensions: Garchomp figure in this 831-piece building toy stands over 6 in. (15 cm) tall
    • Includes 2 SMART Tags and 2 Pokémon
  • Price: USD 79.99 / EUR 79.99 / GBP 69.99

LEGO Pokémon SMART Play: Cubone and Gengar's Spooky Showdown (72166)

LEGO SMART Play™: Cubone and Gengar's Spooky Showdown

  • Pieces: 782
  • Dimensions: Gengar building toy in this 782-piece building toy measures over 4.5 in. (11 cm) high, 4.5 in. (12 cm) wide and 5 in. (13 cm) deep
    • Includes 4 SMART Tags and 2 Pokémon
  • Price: USD 89.99 / EUR 89.99 / GBP 79.99

Tom Phillips is IGN's News Editor. You can reach Tom at tom_phillips@ign.com or find him on Bluesky @tomphillipseg.bsky.social

item.title: Toy Story: Retro Roundup! announced for PS5, Xbox Series, Switch 2, PS4, Switch, and PC
item.content:encoded:
Publisher Atari and developer Digital Eclipse have announced Toy Story: Retro Roundup!, a “definitive collection” of classic Toy Story games, for PlayStation 5, Xbox Series, Switch 2, PlayStation 4, Switch, and PC (Steam). It will launch on October 15. A physical bundle including the newly announced Toy Story 3 Complete Edition will be available for PlayStation 5, Switch 2, PlayStation 4, and Switch. Pre-orders are available now.

Here is an overview of the collection, via Atari:

About

Toy Story: Retro Roundup! is the definitive collection of classic games based on the film franchise, including Toy Story (1995), Toy Story 2: Buzz Lightyear to the Rescue! (1999), Toy Story 2 (1999), Buzz Lightyear of Star Command (2000), and Toy Story Racer (2001), along with a bonus game—A Bug’s Life (1998). The collection brings together titles originally released across PlayStation, Super Nintendo Entertainment System, Game Boy and other platforms, many of which have been unavailable or difficult to access on modern hardware.

  • ❮
  • ❯

Key Features

  • Rewind and Retry Instantly – Roll back gameplay to retry difficult sections without restarting.
  • Save and Load Anytime – Pick up and play exactly where you left off, and save progress when needed.
  • Customize Gameplay Experience With Rex’s Cheat Code – Use Rex’s cheat codes to immediately unlock characters, levels, and bonus play modes including invincibility and unlimited lives.
  • Learn With Practice Mode – Learn how to play by watching guided playthroughs or playing in practice mode.
  • Modernized “How to Play” Guides – Get straight to the action with simplified, fully localized instructions adapted for modern systems.
  • Enhanced Visuals With Classic Options – Play in upgraded, high resolution that brings the original look to life, or switch to the original PlayStation 1 presentation for a nostalgic feel.

In addition to the games themselves, Toy Story: Retro Roundup! includes extensive archival content, with all-new behind the scenes interview featuring rare development materials, archival imagery and insights into both the films and games (including A Bug’s Life).

Players can also enjoy six behind-the-scenes video featurettes with industry voices that offer a deeper look at the franchise’s creative journey, including Jason Katz—head of story, Pixar; Luigi Priore—vice president and general manager, Disney and Pixar Games; Jon Burton—founder, TT Games; and Jim Hanks—actor. In addition, a music player is also included, featuring soundtracks from all games in the collection, including an uncompressed, CD-quality version of the Toy Story (1995) game soundtrack.

Watch the announcement trailer below. View the first screenshots at at the gallery.

Announce Trailer



https://www.gematsu.com/2026/06/toy-story-retro-roundup-announced-for-ps5-xbox-series-switch-2-ps4-switch-and-pc
